coredata.dc:description: Trace detection of nitrite in food has gained increasing attention due to its connection with biological and environmental processes. Herein, a dual-mode fluorometric/colorimetric sensor is established for nitrite detection by combining the oxidase-like activity of iron alkoxide (IA) with the bimodal excitation properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). IA oxidizes the colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine into blue-colored 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine oxide. Subsequently, nitrite further triggers a diazotization reaction, resulting in a color change from blue to green. Simultaneously, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 470 nm decreases significantly, while the signal at 371 nm remains nearly unchanged, owing to the localized inner filter effect. Satisfactory results are attained with low limits of detection of 49 nM in the fluorometric mode and 73 nM in the colorimetric mode. Furthermore, an intelligent lateral flow assay platform is established for the visual detection of nitrite. Therefore, this work provides innovative guidance for nitrite detection in food safety.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trace detection of nitrite in food has gained increasing attention due to its connection with biological and environmental processes. Herein, a dual-mode fluorometric/colorimetric sensor is established for nitrite detection by combining the oxidase-like activity of iron alkoxide (IA) with the bimodal excitation properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). IA oxidizes the colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine into blue-colored 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine oxide. Subsequently, nitrite further triggers a diazotization reaction, resulting in a color change from blue to green. Simultaneously, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 470 nm decreases significantly, while the signal at 371 nm remains nearly unchanged, owing to the localized inner filter effect. Satisfactory results are attained with low limits of detection of 49 nM in the fluorometric mode and 73 nM in the colorimetric mode. Furthermore, an intelligent lateral flow assay platform is established for the visual detection of nitrite. Therefore, this work provides innovative guidance for nitrite detection in food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 −) is a naturally occurring nitrogen species found in aquatic ecosystems (Rommozzi et al., 2020). It is also widely used in the food industry as a preservative antioxidant (Guo, Li, et al., 2023). However, excessive or long-term consumption of NO2 − can lead to acute poisoning, as well as esophageal and gastric cancers, posing seriuos health risks (Zhang et al., 2018). In the human body, NO2 − can react in the stomach to form carcinogenic N-nitrosamines via interaction with amides or secondary amines and can bind to haemoglobin to form methemoglobin, leading to hypoxia (Wu et al., 2020). To address these risks, China has set a maximum allowable NO2 − level of 2 mg·kg−1 (or 94 μM) in food, while the World Health Organization has set a limit of 3 mg·L−1 (or 65 μM) in groundwater (Noori et al., 2022). Therefore, developing reliable and convenient methods for NO2 − detection is essential for ensuring food safety and human health.

Until now, traditional methods have been used to quantify NO2 −, including chromatography, capillary electrophoresis and chemiluminescence (Liu, Xiong, et al., 2023; Sinha & Mukherji, 2024; Xiao et al., 2024). Although these methods have their advantages and provide valuable insights, they still have some shortcomings, such as long analysis times, the need for skilled personnel and complex sample preparation, which severely limit their efficiency and practicality. In contrast, fluorescence and colorimetric have emerged as promising alternatives, standing out for their operational simplicity and rapid result generation (Deng et al., 2024; Lima et al., 2024). Recent advances in nanotechnology have further enhanced its potential (Bhatt et al., 2024). Among various nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention due to their excellent fluorescent properties, chemical stability, water solubility and low toxicity (Ðorđević et al., 2022). To date, various techniques for NO2 − detection based on single-emission CDs have been developed. However, such probes are vulnerable to external interference from biological matrices, instrumentation variability and environmental conditions. Ratiometric fluorescence techniques offer a powerful solution to these limitations by enabling internal correction of environmental effects and signal fluctuations. For instance, Zhan et al. reported a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on green-emitting CDs with a quenching peak at around 500 nm for accurate NO2 − detection (Zhan et al., 2019), while Wu and Tong synthesized dual-emission CDs (373 nm and 545 nm) for similar applications (Wu & Tong, 2020). Similarly, Xiang et al. created a dual-emission nanosensor by encapsulating CDs within dye-doped silica nanoparticles for NO2 − detection (Xiang et al., 2018). Nevertheless, most fluorescent sensors focus only on emission-based detection with limited exploration of fluorescence excitation data. This constrains sensor diversity and innovation. Bimodal excitation-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing offers a promising approach to overcome these limitations by correcting external interferences and expanding the versatility of quantum dots-based detection. Additionally, dual-mode detection that integrates fluorescence and colorimetric signals is gaining popularity due to improved accuracy and data richness. Therefore, the development of a CDs-based dual-mode fluorometric and colorimetric detection method provides a forward-looking and technically demanding avenue for the accurate detection of NO2 −.

Nowadays, nanoenzymes are widely used in colorimetric assays under mild conditions due to their high catalytic stability, low cost and sustainability, making them valuable tools across various fields, especially when combined with CDs for detection. In particular, iron alkoxide (IA), a novel category of nanoenzyme material containing both divalent and trivalent metal irons, has attracted considerable attention for its simple preparation and outstanding catalytic activity. For instance, Xu et al. established a visual quantitative colorimetric method for trace analysis of arsenate in drinking water, utilizing a novel IA-based sensor that produces a color change (Xu et al., 2020). Ding et al. created a sensor for the trace detection of ofloxacin by triggering the release of IA through aptamer-gated mesoporous silica nanoparticles (Ding et al., 2024). However, the combination of IA with CDs for NO2 − detection has not yet been reported. By integrating the fluorescence properties of CDs with the oxidase-like (OXD) activity of IA, a multifunctional CDs@IA composite can be designed for dual-mode detection. Additionally, lateral flow assay (LFA), an excellent detection platform, provides a visual test-effective mean for rapid and field-based detection. Therefore, combining CDs with IA within a LFA framework represents a novel strategy for dual-mode visual detection of NO2 −.

Inspired by the above aspects, we hypothesize that the nitrogen-doped carbon dots conjugated with OXD-like IA (N-CDs@IA) will exhibit modulated OXD-like activity in the presence of NO2 −, leading to specific spectral changes that enable dual-mode fluorescence and colorimetric detection. As shown in Scheme 1 , the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are synthesized via a simple solvothermal method using melamine and o-phenylenediamine as precursor. Then, N-CDs@IA hybrid is prepared by mixing and stirring the prepared N-CDs with IA. Initially, the as-prepared IA, which exhibits outstanding OXD-like catalytic activity, promotes the oxidation of the colorless chromogenic substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine oxide (TMB+) by forming a superoxide-anion radicals from dissolved O2. Upon introducing NO2 −, the produced TMB+ interacts specifically with NO2 − to form diazotized TMB+, causing a visible color change from blue to green under daylight. This change corresponds to a gradual increase in the absorption peak at 457 nm and a decrease at 652 nm. Meanwhile, due to the localization of inner filter effect (L-IFE) between N-CDs and diazotized TMB+, the photoluminescence excitation (PLE) peak of N-CDs at 470 nm decreases faster than that at 371 nm, eventually disappearing. Thus, a dual-mode detection method is designed for sensitive detection of NO2 − detection. Additionally, for rapid identification and preliminary on-site screening, the N-CDs/LFA enables visual detection of NO2 − in actual samples. Hence, this dual-mode fluorescence and colorimetric assay, based on the modulation of OXD-like activity of N-CDs@IA, offers a simple, fast, sensitive and reliable method for NO2 − detection with great potential for practical applications.

Analytical-grade chemical reagents were purchased and did not require further purification. Aladdin (Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) supplied various chemicals, including o-phenylenediamine (OPD, 99.5 %), melamine (99 %), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.5 %), ethanol (≥99.8 %), FeCl3·6H2O, urea, glacial acetic acid, AlCl3, CoCl2, KCl, CuCl2, CdCl2, ZnCl2, MgCl2, CeCl3, AgCl3, HgCl2, BaCl2, NaCl, PbCl2, serine (Ser), L-Asparagine (Asn), proline (Pro), alanine (Ala), tetracycline (TC), pefloxacin (PEF), ofloxacin (OFL), NaNO3, NaNO2, ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GHS), catechol, hydroquinone (Hq). Deionized water utilized in all experiments was provided by the Millipore system (Millipore Corporation, Massachusetts, USA). The salami and pickle were purchased from a food store close to Ningbo University (Ningbo, China).

The microstructure and particle sizes of N-CDs dispersions were characterized using atomic force microscopy (AFM, Asylum Research MFP-3D, USA), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, HitachiSU-70, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan). Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw inVia, Britain) was used to study the molecular structure. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) was employed to determin surface chemistry and functional groups. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, PerkinElmer Spectrum 100, USA) was used to analyze the surface chemical characteristics of N-CDs. A UV-5800 spectrophotometer was used to record ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra. A luminescence spectrometer (Agilent Cary 60, USA) was used to acquire fluorescence spectra, including photoluminescence (PL) and PLE. An Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrometer was used to measure the fluorescence lifetime and relative fluorescence quantum yield of N-CDs at room temperature. All digital images in this work were captured using an iPhone 14.

The N-CDs were synthesized using a solvothermal method as described in the literature (Guo, Wang, et al., 2023). Specifically, 1 mmol of melamine and 1 mmol of OPD were dissolved in 20 mL of DMF via sonication for 15 min. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 10 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was first filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove large particles, followed by dialysis against deionized water for 48 h using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 500 Da to remove unreacted melamine, OPD and small-molecule byproducts. The dialyzed solution was subsequently evaporated under reduced pressure for 24 h. The final product was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The QY value of N-CDs was evaluated based on a previously reported method, using Rhodamine 6G as the reference standard. N-CDs solutions at various concentrations were prepared, ensuring the absorbance values remained between 0.0 and 0.1. The QY was calculated using the following equation (Du et al., 2018):
(1)
QY =
Q ST
I X
/
I ST
K X
/
K ST
η X
/
η ST
2
where I stands for the intensity of emission integral, K indicates the absorbance, and η represents the refractive index of the solvent (1.36 for ethanol). The subscript “ST” and “X” refer to the standard (Rhodamine 6G) and the sample, respectively.

IA was synthesized with slight modifications to a previously reported study (Xu et al., 2020). A mixture of 30 mL of ethylene glycol, 0.2 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 0.9 g of urea was stirred at room temperature for 20 min. The reaction was subsequently carried out at 195 °C for 30 min. The resulting IA particles were progressively accumulated by centrifugation after heating and were washed three times with ethanol and deionized water. The purified particles were air-dried in a vacuum dryer.

The OXD-like activity of IA was evaluated by determination of chromogenic reactions between TMB and IA. Briefly, 0.2 mM NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 4) was mixed with 1 mg of IA and 20 μL of 3.0 μM TMB. After a 120 s incubation, UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded using a spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bimodal ratiometric fluorometric/colorimetric measurement of NO2 −
ce:section.ce:para: The dual-mode ratiometric fluorometric/colorimetric method was validated by measuring different concentrations of NO2 −. Under optimal conditions, 20 μL of TMB solution was mixed with IA and NaAc-HAc buffer. After reacting for 2 min, various concentrations of NO2 − solution (0–600 μM) were added and allowed to react for 3 min. Subsequently, 60 μL of 2.40 mg/mL N-CDs solution was added, and the mixture was incubated for 10 min. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence signals were then measured. Selectivity was examined by introducing a variety of potential interfering substances (Al3+, Co2+, K+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ce3+, Ag+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Na+, Pb2+), amino acids (Ser, Asn, Pro, Ala), antibiotics (TC, PET, OFL), oxidizing agents (NO3 −), reducing agents (AA, GHS) and phenolic compounds (catechol, Hq), followed by measurement of the corresponding fluorescence spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of LFA based on N-CDs and IA
ce:section.ce:para: The LFA test strips were assembled using a nitrocellulose membrane, conjugate pad, absorbent pad and sample pad, all adhered to a polyvinyl chloride backing with a 2 mm overlap. At a dispensing rate of 1 μL/cm, starch (control line, C line), and a mixture of IA, TMB and N-CDs (test line, T line, including T1 and T2) were applied to the nitrocellulose membrane. The strips were dried overnight at 37 °C, cut to 3.5 mm in width and assembled into LFA devices, which were stored in a dry environment.

Semi-quantitative analysis was carried out by visual inspection, while the advanced analysis was performed using a smartphone-based sensing platform comprising an optical module, detector chamber and sample tray. Color images of the strips were captured and analyzed using ColourCard app on an iPhone 14.

Samples of salami and pickles were selected to validate the performance of filtered N-CDs in real samples. After sonication for 15 min, the samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. Subsequently, various concentrations of NO2 − were spiked into the filtered solutions and mixed thoroughly for further analysis. The results were compared with those obtained using the Griess reagent colorimetric method via a related t-test. All experiments were conducted in triplicate, and the mean values were used for further analysis.

TEM and AFM are employed to study the morphological and structural characteristics of the prepared N-CDs. The TEM image (Fig. 1(a)) shows that N-CDs are well-dispersed with a punctiform morphology and show no discernible aggregation. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (inset of Fig. 1(a)) displays distinct lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.215 nm, attributed to the (100) plane of gaphite carbon (Yang et al., 2019). Moreover, the particle size of N-CDs is distributed in the range of 4.0–8.0 nm, with an average diameter of 5.94 nm, as seen in Fig. 1(b). The AFM image (Fig. 1(c)) indicates that the topographical height of N-CDs lies mainly within the range of 1.24–1.87 nm, suggesting the N-CDs primarily consist of a few thick graphene-like layers (Li, Guo, Xing, Wang, et al., 2023). XRD analysis further confirms the graphitic nature of N-CDs, showing a diffraction peak centered at 22.6° in the spectrum in Fig. 1(d) (Zhao et al., 2022).

With FT-IR and XPS analyses, the functional groups and chemical constitution of the N-CDs are investigated. FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 1(e) shows stretching vibrations of -NH/OH (3100–3500 cm−1) and CO (1651 cm−1), indicating that the presence of polar functional groups, such as -NH2, -OH and -COOH, which guarantee the solubility of N-CDs in polar solvents (Ansi & Renuka, 2018). The tensile vibrations of CO (1651 cm−1), CN (1550 cm−1) and CN (1436 cm−1) show that sp2/sp3 hybridized aromatic molecules are present in N-CDs (Xu et al., 2021). Additionally, a moderate peak at 1028 cm−1 is attributed to NH bending vibrations from amine moieties, while the signal at 1195 cm−1 arises from OH bending of surface-bound water molecules, confirming that the presence of hydrogen-containing functional groups is critical for the hydrophilicity and structural integrity of the material (Long et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2022). In the full XPS spectrum (Fig. 1(f)), three main peaks at 284.1 eV, 399.1 eV and 532.1 eV correspond to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively. Furthermore, the atomic percentages of C, N and O are calculated to be 76.85 %, 7.94 % and 15.21 %, respectively (Fig. S1) (Liu, Zhan, et al., 2023). In the high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1(g)), three distinct peaks at 283.2 eV, 284.8 eV and 287.2 eV are ascribed to C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N and C=O/C=N, respectively (Song et al., 2017). The N 1 s spectrum in Fig. 1(h) shows four peaks at 394.0 eV, 397.9 eV, 400.1 eV and 405.0 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, amino N and graphitic N, respectively, which are part of the π-conjugated graphene-like structure (Wang, Sun, et al., 2024). Similarly, the O 1 s spectrum shows two peaks at 530.4 eV and 531.9 eV (Fig. 1(i)), confirming the presence of CO and CO bonds (Jayaweera et al., 2019). Based on the FT-IR and XPS results, the synthesized N-CDs consist of a conjugated sp2/sp3-C core, modified by sp3 hybridized carbon-containing groups. This unique structure imparts N-CDs with exceptional fluorescence properties, forming a robust foundation for NO2 − detection.

The optical properties of the N-CDs are investigated through UV–vis, PLE and PL spectra. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the UV–vis spectrum displays absorption bands at approximately 287 nm and within the 350–460 nm range. These bonds are associated with π-π* transitions of the aromatic sp2 carbon domains (C=C) and n-π* transitions related to surface states (Jiang et al., 2019). Under excitation at 371 nm and 470 nm, the N-CDs solution emits green fluorescence with an emission peak at around 533 nm (λem). Under 365 nm UV light, the N-CDs solution emits green fluorescence and appears brown under daylight. Fig. 2(b) illustrate that the emission wavelength remains unchanged while the fluorescence intensity varies as the excitation wavelength increases from 340 to 480 nm. This excitation-independent behavior is indicative of the consistent structural distribution and uniform surface states in N-CDs. The three-dimensional fluorescence map of N-CDs in Fig. 2(c) reveals two excitation centers, indicating dual excitation pathways leading to same emission. Meanwhile, the QY of N-CDs in aqueous solution, calculated using Rhodamine 6G as the reference, is approximately 29.24 %. The fluorescence stability of N-CDs under extented storage, exposure to daylight and varying ion strengths (NaCl concentrations) is also examined. Fig. 2(d-e) illustrate that no significant loss in PL intensity is observed after UV exposure for 24 h or storage for 30 days, indicating the excellent photostability and stability of N-CDs. Additionally, the PL intensity of N-CDs remains stable even at a NaCl concentration of 2 mol/L (Fig. 2(f)). These findings collectively highlight N-CDs have robust optical properties and environmental stability, underscoring their strong potential for use in fluorescent probe-based detection systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of dual-mode fluorometric and colorimetric detection of NO2 −
ce:section.ce:para: Numerous investigations have been conducted to understand the underlying mechanism of the dual-mode NO2 − sensor. In general, metal ions and alcohol molecules chelate to generate metal alkoxides, and follows the same principle. According to the SEM image in Fig. S2(a), IA exhibits an approximately spherical morphology with a diameter of 1.8 μm. Additionally, numerous tiny and convoluted nanosheets on the surface form a flower-like structure. The XRD pattern of the prepared IA (Fig. S2(b)) shows a strong diffraction peak at 2θ = 11°, which is consistent with the reported data (Li et al., 2011). Furthermore, the XPS spectra of IA in Fig. S2(c-f) confirm its chemical structure and successful synthesis, displaying the presence of C, O and Fe. To investigate the OXD-like activity of IA, its chromogenic interaction with TMB is analyzed. Typically, Michaelis-Menton constant (Km) and maximum initial rate (Vmax) are used to assess the OXD-like activity of IA (Fig. S3). The Km and Vmax values are calculated from Lineweaver-Burk plots to be 0.12 mM and 2.11 × 10−8 M·s−1, respectively. Compared with some existing OXD-like nanoenzymes, the lower Km and higher Vmax of IA imply higher catalytic efficiency (Table S1). The enhaced activity is in part attributed to the flower-like structure greatly, which increases the specific surface area and promotes faster conversion of dissolved oxygen into O2 ●-, thereby facilitating electron transfer between TMB and TMB+.

Based on the foregoing observations, the proposed colorimetric detection system operates via two key steps: (1) the oxidation of TMB to TMB+ by IA; (2) the diazotization of TMB+ in the presence of NO2 −. Fig. 3(a) illustrates that neither IA (curve i) nor TMB (curve ii) exhibit absorption peaks at 652 nm. However, the TMB + IA system shows a pronounced absorption peak at 652 nm, indicating the formation of blue TMB+ (curve iii) (Luo et al., 2024). IA acts as an oxidase mimic, activating dissolved oxygen to form superoxide radicals, which in turn oxidize colorless TMB to blue TMB+. Upon the addition of NO2 −, the aromatic primary amine group of TMB+ undergoes diazotization. This reaction enhances the absorption peak at 457 nm and diminishes the peak at 652 nm (curve iv). Moreover, it changes the solution color from blue to green, offering a strong basis for NO2 − detection under daylight. Besides, the IA-based nanozyme approach eliminates the potential health risks associated with H2O2, highlighting both the safety and practicality.

In addition to the color change in solution, the proposed fluorescence detection mechanism relies on the interaction between the N-CDs and the generated diazotized TMB+, which quenches the fluorescence of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S4, the initial zeta potential of N-CDs is measured at −34.2 mV, which increased to −11.5 mV upon NO2 − addition. This shift towards a less negative value suggests partial charge neutralization due to NO2 − adsorption, which reduces electrostatic repulsion and facilitates molecular interactions (Hao et al., 2022). The binding constant (K) is calculated to confirm the interaction strength between N-CDs and targets through the Benesi-Hildebrand equation (Yang, Jia, Hou, Zhao, & Wang, 2022):
(2)
1 /
F −
F 0
= 1 / K
F s
−
F 0
C + 1 /
F s
−
F 0
where F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the absence and presence of an analyte, Fs and C represent the fluorescence intensity of saturated analyte and the concentration of analyte, respectively. As presented in Fig. S5, the K value of N-CDs with diazotized TMB+ is calculated to be 4.4 × 104 mol−1, illustrating that more intense interactions exist between them. Additionally, the UV–vis absorbances spectra of N-CDs (curve i), diazotized TMB+ (curve ii), N-CDs@diazotized TMB+ blend (curve iii) and the sum of curve i and curve ii (curve iv) are displayed in Fig. 3(b). The absence of peak displacement in curve iii compared to curve iv suggests that N-CDs and diazotized TMB+ do not form a complex or covalent bond. Fig. 3(c) shows the PLE spectra of N-CDs under different conditions, highlighting that only diazotized TMB+ can quench the PLE of N-CDs at 470 nm. Fig. 3(d) reveals the 3D mapping of N-CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of diazotized TMB+. The strong overlap between the absorption peak of diazotized TMB+ and the PLE of N-CDs, along with a narrower overlap with the PL, supports the occurrence of IFE and FRET quenching (Li, Guo, Xing, Tang, et al., 2023). It is well known that the fluorescence lifetime of luminescent CDs remains constant under IFE but varies during FRET (Tang et al., 2022). Flourescence lifetime measurements (Fig. 3(e-f)) reveal negligible variation in the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs with and without diazotized TMB+, whether excited at 371 nm or 470 nm (Table S2). This confirms that IFE is the primary mechanism of fluorescence quenching. The uneven overlap of the excitation spectrum of N-CDs and the absorption spectra of diazotized TMB+ indicates that L-IFE is the predominant form of fluorescence quenching. To verify this conclusion, the Parker equations are used to calculate the IFE efficiencies (Wang et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2015):

The parameter h indicates the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette (0.4 cm), d represents the width of the excitation beam (0.1 cm), and l refers to the width of the cuvette (1.0 cm). These results indicate that the quenching efficiency of IFE increases with rising NO2 − concentration, with IFE emerging as the dominant mechanism driving fluorescence quenching in this system. Notably, the quenching efficiency at an excitation wavelength of 371 nm is much lower than that observed at 471 nm, suggesting that the primary quenching mechanism is attributed to L-IFE (Zhai et al., 2014). Fig. 3(g) shows a detailed schematic diagram illustrating the corresponding sensing mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-mode colorimetric/fluorometric ratio detection of NO2 −
ce:section.ce:para: From the aforementioned study, it is evident that colorless TMB is oxidized to blue TMB+ catalyzed by IA and subsequently converted to green diazotized TMB+ in the presence of NO2 −. Meanwhile, the dual excitation characteristics at 371 nm and 470 nm are selectively quenched under emission at 533 nm. Based on these phenomena, the N-CDs@IA sensing system can be utilized for dual-mode detection of NO2 − through both colorimetric and fluorometric sensors.

To optimize the catalytic efficiency of IA in transforming colorless TMB into blue TMB+, the influence of buffer pH is examined. Fig. 4(a) reveals that the TMB + IA reactive system exhibits maximum absorbance at 652 nm at pH = 4, where the color change is most pronounced. Consequently, all subsequent chromogenic reactions are carried out under such conditions. Next, the effect of IA concentration on the test outcome is examined. As illustrated in Fig. 4(b), the catalytic effect increases with increasing IA concentration, reaching a plateau at concentrations exceeding 80 μM. Therefore, the IA concentration is set at 80 μM. Similarly, the influence of TMB concentration is assessed, as depicted in Fig. 4(c), with optimal performance observed at 3.0 μM. These optimized conditions enable efficient and accurate real-time monitoring of NO2 −.

The color change and UV–vis absorption spectra of the solutions are recorded. As clearly seen in Fig. 4(d), the absorbance at 652 nm gradually decreases, while the absorbance at 457 nm progressively increases with rising NO2 − concentrations, corresponding to a discernible color shift from blue to green (inset in Fig. 3(g)). Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 4(e), the intensity ratio of A 457/A 652 (where A 457 and A 652 represent the UV–vis absorption intensities of N-CDs at 457 nm and 652 nm, respectively) increases with NO2 − concentration increasing from 0 to 600 μM. As clearly shown in Fig. 4(f), the linear regression equation for NO2 − concentrations varying from 0.073 to 200 μM is given by Y = 0.0345× + 0.3898 (R 2 = 0.9874), where X represents the NO2 − concentration, and Y represents the A 457/A 652 of the response mixture. Additionally, the LOD is determined to be 73 nM using the equation LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution, and k is the slope of the calibration curve. These findings suggest that N-CDs show significant promise for colorimetric detection of NO2 −.

Similarly, for fluorometric detection of NO2 −, key parameters affecting sensing performance also need to be optimized. As depicted in Fig. 5(a), the PL intensity of the N-CDs@IA system exhibits minimal variation after sustained thermal equilibration, maintaning relative stability up to 60 °C. Additionally, the quenching efficiency increases with rising pH, followed by a decline to a constant value (Fig. 5(b)). Based on these observations, the optimal pH value for NO2 − sensing is approximately 4. Next, the response time of the sensor to NO2 − is investigated, revealing that the reactivity of N-CDs@IA to NO2 − stabilizes after 120 s (Fig. 5(c)). Subsequently, the PLE spectra of N-CDs with varying concentrations are examined to determine the optimal conditions for NO2 − detection. Fig. 5(d) shows that the initial wide excitation peak splits into two distinct peaks when the N-CDs concentration reaches a critical point. The corresponding 3D fluorescence map of N-CDs solution with different concentrations to NO2 − is shown more clearly in Fig. S6. Meanwhile, Fig. 5(e) illustrates that the emission peak redshifts from 491 to 562 nm. By adjusting the concentration of N-CDs, the distance between the two excitation peaks can be finely tuned (Fig. S7 and Table S5), facilitating the overlap of the absorbance of diazotized TMB+ with the excitation of N-CDs at long wavelengths. Based on these findings, Fig. 5(f) shows that the quenching efficiency varies with N-CDs concentration, with 2.40 mg/mL being the optimal concentration for NO2 − ratio sensing. Overall, these optimized conditions present a promising avenue for further investigation.

Following this, a novel fluorescence detection method is developed using the different responses of the two excitation peaks to NO2 −. Notably, as seen in Fig. 5(g), with increasing NO2 − concentration, the PLE intensity of N-CDs at 470 nm progressively decreases, which is significantly more pronounced than that at 371 nm. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5(h), the intensity ratio of I 371/I 470 (I 371 and I 470 represent the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at 371 nm and 470 nm, respectively) increases as NO2 − concentration rises from 0 to 600 μM. Fig. 5(i) clearly reveals a strong linea relationship (R 2 = 0.9822) over the NO2 − concentration ranging from 0.049 to 150 μM, with the fitted linear equation of Y = 0.0029× + 0.7824 (X represents the NO2 − concentration, and Y is the I 371/I 470 of reaction mixture). Additionally, the LOD is determined to be 49 nM. Upon the addition of NO2 − solution, the bright green fluorescence of N-CDs remains largely unchanged when excited at 371 nm, whereas the fluorescence under 470 nm excitation decreases gradually, which is visually apparent (inset in Fig. 3(g)). Therefrom, this dual-signal-based fluorometric/colorimetric strategy can be exploited for accurate and effective monitoring of NO2 − in complex environments (Table S6).

To assess the selectivity of the fluorescent probe for NO2 − detection, the effects of common metal ions (Al3+, Co2+, K+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ce3+, Ag+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Na+, Pb2+), amino acids (Ser, Asn, Pro, Ala), antibiotics (TC, PET, OFL), oxidizing agents (NO3 −), reducing agents (AA, GHS) and phenolic compounds (catechol, Hq) are investigated. As shown in Fig. S8, it is evident that only NO2 − can significantly inhibits the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, while the effects of other potential interfering substances are negligible, suggesting that N-CDs exhibit high selectivity for NO2 − detection. Upon mixing with the aforementioned interfering substances, the relative PLE intensities of the N-CDs@IA sensing system remain largely unchanged, indicating its resistance to interference. These findings confirm the exceptional selectivity of the N-CDs@IA sensing system for NO2 − detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Device for dual-recognition analysis to identify NO2 −
ce:section.ce:para: To enable on-site detection of NO2 −, a smartphone-integrated LFA-based fluorescence/colorimetric analysis platform is designed, ensuring both the reliability and accuracy. The sensing process involves two key modules: the LFA imaging platform and the smartphone analysis. Firstly, the LFA strip is constructed based on its working mechanism (Fig. 6(a)), comprising a nitrocellulose membrane, conjugate pad, absorbent pad and sample pad, all affixed to a polyvinyl chloride (PVC) backing. Upon application, the sample solution flows from the sample pad to the conjugate pad, triggering the relevant reaction and precipitation. Subsequently, image processing, including signal conversion, image acquisition and quantitative analysis, are carried out using a smartphone. Test images are captured using the smartphone camera, and the color signals are digitally concerted into precise red (R), green (G) and blue (B) values using the ColourCard application. In this case, the T1 line is detected at 371 nm, the T2 line at 470 nm, while the C line is visible under daylight after the sample passes through the nitrocellulose membrane. The interaction between KI and starch is generates a blue C line, comfirming proper development of the T line (Fig. 6(b)). To validate the method, real samples including salami and pickles are analyzed using the N-CDs/LFA platform (Fig. 6(c)). For accuarcy verification, the Griess analysis is employed as a reference method. After the addition of the sample, both T1 and T2 lines exhibit significant changes under UV and daylight. Subsequently, the ratios of fluorescence intensities (Sum1/Sum2) and colorimetric values (G/B) are calculated using the application software. As shown in Fig. S9, both ratios show a strong linear relationships with NO2 − concentration ranging from 0 to 160 μM, with calibration equations of Sum1/Sum2 = 0.0036× + 0.7719 (R2 = 0.9974) and G/B = 0.0159× + 0.5247 (R2 = 0.9985), where X represents the NO2 − concentration. The corresponding LODs are 87 nM and 124 nM, respectively. Relative standard deviations (RSDs) are less than 1.83 %, and the spiking recovery rates range from 98.9 % to 101.3 %, as shown in Table S6. Notably, results obtained from the Griess method show strong agreement with those derived from the N-CDs/LFA platform, confirming its reliability for practical applications. Moreover, the t-test at the 95 % confidence level indicates no appreciable difference between the two methods, further demonstrating the analytical robustness of the proposed platform. These findings confirm that the portable LFA-based platform provides a rapid, user-friendly and sensitive approach for on-site NO₂− detection with high accuracy and dependability.

In summary, a dual-mode fluorometric/colorimetric probe based on N-CDs@IA is developed for the efficient detection of NO2 −. N-CDs are synthesized via a hydrothermal process using melamine and OPD as precursors, resulting in bright green fluorescence and excellent optical stability. IA is then incorporated due to its excellent OXD-like activity, which facilitates the oxidation of colorless TMB to blue TMB+. Upon the addition of NO2 −,diazotization reactions further conver TMB+ to diazotized TMB+, leading to a visible color shift from blue to green. Meanwhile, L-IFE causes a pronounced fluorescence quenching of N-CDs at 470 nm excitation, while minimal changes is observed at 371 nm excitation. The ratiometric probe, defined by the intensity ratio of A 457/A 652 (I 371/I 470), possesses outstanding selectivity and sensitivity for NO2 − detection, achieving a LOD of 73 nM (or 49 nM) across the linear concentration range of 0.073 to 200 μM (or 0.049 to 150 μM). Additionally, considering the differential responses of colorimetric and fluorescence methods, an intelligent N-CDs@IA-based LFA platform is developed for the visual detection of NO2 − in actual samples. The method achieves recovery rates of 98.9 %–101.3 % with RSDs below 1.83 %. This study not only proposes a novel bifunctional sensing strategy based on N-CDs@IA but also provides a promising approach for extending the application of LFAs in environmental monitoring and food safety analysis.

Dingfeng Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Investigation, Conceptualization. Liwei Gao: Visualization, Supervision, Conceptualization. Xinru Liu: Software, Methodology, Formal analysis. Linfan Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ting Zhang: Supervision, Data curation. Guoqiang Guo: Visualization, Investigation, Conceptualization. Hongxing He: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Da Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.
